nativeRange.habitat: Calm, slow-moving rivers, lakes, ponds, swamps and cypress swamps; often found rooted in soft mud in shallow water with full or partial sun.

decorPreferences: soft mud or fine sand/gravel substrate
space above for floating leaves to develop

avoidWith: vegetarian plecos that uproot or shred soft leaves

summary: The Banana Plant (Nymphoides aquatica) is a native North American aquatic plant named for the cluster of banana-shaped storage tubers at its base. Beginner-friendly and adaptable, it first produces small submerged leaves before sending long stems to the surface to develop floating, lily-pad-style leaves.
careNotes:
The Banana Plant is best known for the cluster of swollen, banana-shaped storage tubers at its base — these are NOT roots and should NOT be buried. Rest the plant on the substrate so the tubers sit at or only just below the surface; burying them more than about one-third of their depth is generally associated with rot. True white roots emerge from the crown above the tubers and anchor the plant into the substrate. As the plant establishes itself, the tubers often shrink and soften once the plant has drawn the stored nutrients from them — this is normal, and not a cause for concern as long as new growth continues.

In its early weeks, the plant sends up short, dark-green submerged leaves on short stems. Once well rooted, it generally pushes long stems to the surface bearing round, heart-shaped floating leaves in the manner of a miniature water lily; these stems can reach the surface from depths of up to roughly 45 cm. Floating leaves can shade other plants underneath and tend to benefit from periodic pruning.

The Banana Plant tolerates a wide range of light — from low to high — with medium light generally regarded as a sensible starting point. Low light tends to keep growth slow and the plant compact and submerged, while brighter light encourages faster growth and the development of surface leaves. CO2 supplementation is not required, though it generally speeds growth.

Propagation is most reliably done by cutting a mature floating leaf with a portion of its stem and allowing it to float freely at the surface; new roots and small plantlets typically form at the base of the cut stem within a few weeks and can then be pressed gently into the substrate. The species is native to the southeastern United States, ranging from Texas to Maryland and found most abundantly in Florida swamps and slow-moving waters; it is listed as endangered in Maryland. Individual plants have been reported to live well for three to five years before declining, and growers often replace stock periodically as plants age.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica search page reached but the species-specific detail page returned an XSLT parsing error during research. Cross-source data was assembled from Aquarium Co-Op, AquariumBreeder, FishLab and Wikipedia instead; primary citation retained for the slice's source-priority record.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed beginner care level, temperature 20–28 °C (68–82 °F), tuber-handling guidance (rest on substrate, do not bury), and leaf-cutting propagation method.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 20–26 °C optimal (10–30 °C tolerance), pH 6.0–7.5, gH 1–8 ideal, height 15–50 cm depending on water depth, slow–medium growth, nutrient-rich substrate preferred but adaptable, and native range Texas–Virginia.

additional.name: FishLab
additional.notes: Confirmed pH 6.0–7.2, kH 5–12, temperature 20–27 °C, medium growth, easy care level, propagation by cutting mature lily-pad stalks, tuber-handling rule (bury at most ~1/3 of the tuber).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy verification: family Menyanthaceae, order Asterales; native range southeastern United States; common-name synonyms (banana plant, banana lily, banana floatingheart, big floatingheart).

propagation: leaf cutting (float a trimmed mature leaf with stem until plantlets form)
rest tubers on substrate, do not bury
